name: Lahamu
description: Lahamu is a primordial serpent deity in Babylonian mythology, one of the firstborn gods. Along with her consort Lahmu, she is a child of Apsu (fresh water) and Tiamat (salt water), representing the silt and sediment that formed where the fresh and saltwater met. Lahamu and Lahmu are considered the parents of Anshar and Kishar, who in turn are the parents of Anu (sky), Ea (wisdom/water), and other major deities. Lahamu embodies the fundamental building blocks of the cosmos, representing the foundational elements from which the world and the pantheon of gods emerged. While not actively worshipped in later Babylonian periods, her role as a primordial ancestor solidifies her importance within the creation myth, Enuma Elish. Lahamu is often depicted as a serpentine figure, reflecting her connection to the watery chaos that preceded order. Her power lies in her ancestral status and her representation of the raw, unformed matter from which the world was created. She is a passive force, more a representation of a condition than an active participant in events. Her existence is crucial to understanding the Babylonian worldview, where the universe arose from a primordial soup of opposing forces. Although details about Lahamu are scarce, her essential role within the creation narrative is undeniable, marking her as a cornerstone of Babylonian cosmology. She represents the beginning, the potential for creation, and the raw materials from which everything was formed. The absence of later active worship does not diminish her significance as a primordial ancestor and a fundamental element of the Babylonian creation story.
subtitle: Primordial Serpent Deity of Creation

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Cosmos
keyMyths.description: Lahamu and Lahmu are born from Apsu and Tiamat, representing the mingling of fresh and saltwater. This union creates the primordial silt, marking the genesis of the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: Ancestry of the Gods
keyMyths.description: Lahamu and Lahmu give birth to Anshar and Kishar, who in turn parent the major Babylonian gods like Anu and Ea. This establishes Lahamu's role as a key ancestral figure.

keyMyths.title: Serpent Form
keyMyths.description: Lahamu is often depicted as a serpent, symbolizing her connection to the primordial waters and the chaos that preceded creation. This form reflects her ancient and fundamental nature.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations of Babylonian Art and Texts

sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
